title: Generation of mouse hepatobiliary organoids from hepatocyte progenitors and cholangiocytes isolated from healthy adult mouse liver
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"rvbir","text":"Number of liver organoids have been reported, though it is not clearly shown whether the functional connection between hepatocytes and cholangiocytes is recapitulated in those organoids. Here, we report generation of a hepatobiliary tubular organoid (HBTO) using mouse hepatocyte progenitors called small hepatocytes (SHs) and cholangiocytes. SHs differentiate and form the bile canalicular network in HBTOs and secret metabolites into the canaliculi, which are then transported into the biliary structure. Hepatocytes in the organoid acquire and maintain metabolic functions including albumin secretion and cytochrome P450 activities, over the long term. We provide the step-by-step protocol for induction of HBTO including isolation of cholangiocytes and SHs and co-culture of these two types of cell to generate functional connections between hepatocytes and cholangiocytes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"o2ytv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"94m6i","text":"\tEpithelial organs consist of multiple types of epithelial tissue, such as alveoli and trachea in the lung, urinary tubules and collecting ducts in the kidney, acini and pancreatic ducts in the pancreas, and bile canaliculi (BC) and bile ducts (BDs) in the liver. The structures connecting two types of tissues are unique to each organ. Various substances, including air, urine, digestive enzymes, and bile are altered in composition as they flow through the connecting structures It is therefore crucial to accurately connect tissue structures composed of different types of epithelial cells, in order to reproduce the function of each organ ex vivo.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":642,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"t8t4a","text":"\tTo generate organoids, or mini-organs, tissue stem cells are cultured in three dimensions (3D), to enable the cells to grow, divide, and self-organize into tissue structures similar to those found in vivo. This approach has been used to generate functional components of the gastrointestinal tract, including intestinal (1), gastric (2), colonic (3), and hepatic tissues (4, 5). The organoids are likely to contain niches for maintaining tissue stem/progenitor cells, given that these organoids can expand in size. However, each organoid consists of a single type of tissue, and they do not contain structures connecting different types of epithelial cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":198,"length":7},{"style":"italic","offset":322,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":335,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":348,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":373,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mrced","text":"\tIn this study, we present a mouse hepatobiliary tubular organoid (HBTO) with hepatocyte clusters derived from small hepatocytes (SHs), and a biliary network derived from epithelial cell adhesion molecule positive (EpCAM+) cholangiocytes. In HBTO, bilirubin and fluorescein-labeled bile acid were absorbed by the hepatocytes, excreted into BCs, and then accumulated in the biliary system, indicating that a connection between hepatocytes and cholangiocytes had been established. The development of HBTOs enabled us to investigate the transport of hepatocyte metabolites within the liver tissue, and to monitor the metabolism of the hepatocytes in the long-term ex vivo.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":661,"length":7},{"style":"sup","offset":220,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zrmkf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Anesthetize a mouse with isoflurane
Cut the center of the abdominal wall and expose the portal vein
Insert a butterfly needle into the portal vein and fix it with a clamp
Turn on the peristatic pump and cut the inferior vena cava
Perfuse the liver with 25 ml of pre-perfusion solution at 6 ml/min
Perfused 40 ml of the perfusion solution at 3 ml/min
Transfer the liver to a petri dish
Peel off the liver capsule and dissociate hepatocytes using two tweezers
Transfer the undigested tissue to a 6 cm dish and keep on ice
Cut the undigested tissue into small pieces with scissors
Suspend the tissue pieces in 10 ml of L-15 medium and transfer to a 30 ml beaker containing a stirring bar
Add 30 mg collagenase, 25 ml of hyaluronidase solution, 10 ml of 5 mg/ml DNase I to the beaker
Stir the solution at 37 o C for 40 min
Add 10 ml of DMEM/F12 medium containing 10% FBS to the cell suspension
Pass the cell suspension through a 70 mm cell strainer
Centrifuge at 50 × g for 1 min to eliminate hepatocytes and cell clumps
Centrifuge the cell supernatant at 350 × g for 4 min to collect dissociated cells
Resuspend cells in 200 ml of basal medium
Add 2 ml of anti-CD16/32 (FcgIII/II receptor) antibody and incubated at 4 o C for 30 min
Add 2 ml of chilled wash buffer and centrifuge at 350 × g for 4 min
Resuspend cells in 200 ml basal medium
Add 1 ml of biotin-conjugated anti-EpCAM antibody and incubated at 4 o C for 30 min
Centrifuge the cell supernatant at 350 × g for 4 min to collect dissociated cells
Resuspend cells in 100 ml of MACS buffer
Add 10 ml of streptavidin microbeads and incubated at 4 o C for 15 min
Centrifuge the cell supernatant at 350 × g for 4 min to collect dissociated cells
Resuspend cells in 500 ml of MACS buffer
Isolate EpCAM + cholangiocytes using MACS
Centrifuge the EpCAM + fraction at 350 × g for 4 min to collect cholangiocytes
Count the number of live cells
Resuspend cells in growth medium at the density of 5×10 4 cells/ 300 ml growth medium
Mix 100 ml of DMEM/NaOH solution, high concentration type I collagen, and autoclaved water to make 1 ml of 4 mg/ml collagen solution
If the solution is yellow, add 5~10 ml 0.5N NaOH, near to neutralize
Pour 250 ml collagen solution into each well of a 24 well plate
Incubate the plate at 4 o C for 30 min
Add 500 ml basal medium and incubate 1~2 min at room temperature
Eliminate the medium by aspiration
Feed 5×10 4 cholangiocytes onto each well
Culture for 5 days
Dissociate hepatocytes from the liver by two-step collagenase perfusion
Pass the cell suspension through 250 mm Nylon mesh
Pass the cell suspension through 70 mm cell strainer set on a 50 ml tube
Centrifuge at 50 × g for 1 min and transfer the supernatant to a new 50 ml tube
Centrifuge at 50 × g for 1 min and transfer the supernatant to a new 50 ml tube
Centrifuge at 115 × g for 3 min
Suspend the pellet in 6 ml of HBSS and add 6 ml of Percoll solution
Centrifuged at 180 × g for 15 min to eliminate dead cells
Resuspend cells in 2 ml basal medium and count cells
Resuspend cells in growth medium at the density of 5×10 4 cells/ 300 ml
Eliminate medium from cholangiocyte culture
Feed 5×10 4 SHs onto each well
Place the plate in CO 2 incubator
Replace growth medium with differentiation medium-I at 2 days after SH plating and incubate for 1 day
Eliminate differentiation medium and overlay cells with 200 ml of collagen gel containing 20% MG (Col-MG), which was prepared by mixing 2 mg/ml Collagen IPC and MG (v/v = 4:1) on ice
Incubated the plate at 37°C for 3–4 hours to form a gel
Add 400 ml of differentiation medium-II to each well
Replace medium with fresh one every four days.

l At 1 week after Col-MG overlay, hepatobiliary connections can be recognized by phase contrast microscope (black arrowhead in Fig. 1). l At 2 weeks after Col-MG overlay, bile canaliculi are well developed in hepatocyte clusters (white arrowheads in Fig. 1). Bile canaliculi are more clearly recognized at 3 weeks. l With staining with fluorescent dye-conjugated phalloidin after appropriate fixing, the continuous luminal network can be observed under a fluorescent microscope. To visualize luminal spaces both in hepatocyte clusters and biliary tubules, imaging under a confocal microscope is necessary. l Hepatocyte functions including ALB secretion and CYP activity are gradually increased by two or three weeks after Col-MG overlay. The activities will maintain for more than 2 weeks.

Generation of functional liver organoids on combining hepatocytes and cholangiocytes with hepatobiliary connections ex vivo by Naoki Tanimizu, Norihisa Ichinohe, Yasushi Sasaki, Tohru Itoh, Ryo Sudo, Tomoko Yamaguchi, Takeshi Katsuda, Takafumi Ninomiya, Takashi Tokino, Takahiro Ochiya, Atsushi Miyajima, Toshihiro Mitaka published in Nature Communications on 07 Jun, 2021 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-23575-1
Figure 1. Protocol for induction of heptaobiliary tubular organoid (HBTO) and morphological changes during culture.
creator: Protocols Importer
